patient_explanation: The heart appears larger than normal on the scan.
clinical_context: Seen on chest X-rays. May indicate heart strain, cardiomyopathy, or fluid around the heart.
action_note: Discuss this finding with a healthcare professional.
follow_up: Echocardiogram often recommended

patient_explanation: There is extra fluid in the space around the lungs.
clinical_context: Can result from heart failure, infection, malignancy, or inflammation. May cause breathlessness.
action_note: A doctor can explain the cause and next steps.
follow_up: Thoracentesis may be needed for diagnosis or treatment

simple_meaning: Part of the lung is not fully open
patient_explanation: A small part of the lung is partly collapsed or not fully expanded.
clinical_context: Common post-operatively or due to mucus plugging. Usually temporary and reversible.
action_note: This should be interpreted with symptoms and doctor advice.
follow_up: Breathing exercises or physiotherapy often recommended

patient_explanation: A bone is cracked or broken.
clinical_context: Seen on X-rays after injury. Can be cortical, hairline, comminuted, or pathological.
action_note: Medical review is needed to decide treatment.
follow_up: Orthopaedic consultation and immobilization

simple_meaning: Area of lung filled with infection or fluid
patient_explanation: Part of the lung appears denser than normal, often due to infection or inflammation.
clinical_context: Often seen with pneumonia. Air is replaced by fluid, pus, or cells.
action_note: A healthcare professional can explain the cause and treatment if needed.
follow_up: Antibiotics if infective; repeat imaging may confirm resolution

patient_explanation: Air is trapped around the lung, which can cause part of the lung to collapse.
clinical_context: Can be spontaneous or traumatic. Tension pneumothorax is a medical emergency.
action_note: This should be reviewed urgently by a healthcare professional.
follow_up: Urgent chest drain if large; observation if small

patient_explanation: There is scarring in part of the tissue seen on the scan.
clinical_context: Result of chronic inflammation. Can reduce lung flexibility.
action_note: A doctor can explain whether follow-up is needed.
follow_up: Pulmonologist review; PFTs and HRCT may be considered

patient_explanation: Bleeding has been detected in the scanned area.
clinical_context: In brain imaging this may include subdural, epidural, subarachnoid, or intracerebral bleeding.
action_note: Urgent medical review may be needed.
follow_up: Neurosurgery review; blood pressure control may be needed

patient_explanation: Extra fluid is causing swelling in the tissue.
clinical_context: Can occur in many areas. Significance depends on location and cause.
action_note: The cause should be discussed with a healthcare professional.
follow_up: Depends on location and severity

patient_explanation: An area looks different from normal tissue. It does not always mean cancer.
clinical_context: Broad term covering any focal abnormality. Nature depends on size, location, and imaging features.
action_note: Further evaluation may be recommended.
follow_up: MRI with contrast or biopsy may be needed

patient_explanation: A small spot was seen on the scan.
clinical_context: Lung nodules are commonly monitored depending on size and appearance.
action_note: A doctor may recommend monitoring or additional imaging.
follow_up: Follow-up CT may be recommended depending on guidelines

patient_explanation: There is calcium build-up in the tissue or blood vessel.
clinical_context: Can occur with aging, previous inflammation, or vascular disease.
action_note: This is often a chronic or older finding.
follow_up: Usually no action required unless clinically significant

patient_explanation: The scan shows changes that commonly happen over time with aging or joint use.
clinical_context: Includes osteophytes, disc space narrowing, and subchondral sclerosis. Very common with age.
action_note: A doctor can explain if treatment or exercises may help.
follow_up: Physiotherapy; pain management; orthopaedic review if severe

patient_explanation: The scanned area looks normal and no significant problem was seen.
clinical_context: Standard radiology term for normal findings.
action_note: This is generally reassuring.
follow_up: No action required

patient_explanation: A larger abnormal lump or growth was seen on the scan.
clinical_context: Can be benign or malignant and often needs further characterization.
action_note: This should be discussed with a healthcare professional.
follow_up: Biopsy or further imaging often required

patient_explanation: Part of the lung shows changes that may be due to infection, fluid, or inflammation.
clinical_context: Non-specific term. Often requires clinical correlation.
action_note: A doctor can explain whether treatment or follow-up is needed.
follow_up: Cultures; antibiotics if infective; repeat imaging

patient_explanation: The lungs appear more inflated than usual on the scan.
clinical_context: Indicates air trapping and may be associated with COPD or asthma.
action_note: This should be interpreted with breathing symptoms and medical history.
follow_up: Pulmonary function tests; respiratory physician review

patient_explanation: The lining around the lungs appears thicker than usual.
clinical_context: Can occur after infection, inflammation, exposure, or injury.
action_note: A healthcare professional can explain if follow-up is required.
follow_up: Occupational history important; CT may be needed

simple_meaning: Damage to air spaces in the lungs
patient_explanation: The scan shows changes in the lungs that can make breathing less efficient.
clinical_context: Permanent air-space damage. Often related to smoking or chronic lung irritation.
action_note: Discuss this with a healthcare professional, especially if there are breathing symptoms.
follow_up: Pulmonology referral; smoking cessation; bronchodilators

patient_explanation: There is extra fluid in the lung tissue.
clinical_context: Can be related to heart failure, kidney problems, or fluid overload.
action_note: This should be reviewed promptly by a healthcare professional.
follow_up: Urgent assessment; oxygen/diuretics may be needed

patient_explanation: Some brain structures appear pushed away from their usual position.
clinical_context: Can happen when swelling, bleeding, or pressure pushes brain structures.
action_note: This usually needs urgent medical review.
follow_up: Neurosurgical emergency; ICP monitoring may be needed

patient_explanation: An area may not be getting enough blood supply.
clinical_context: In brain imaging, this can relate to stroke-like changes.
action_note: This should be reviewed by a healthcare professional.
follow_up: Stroke team assessment may be needed

simple_meaning: Tissue damage from reduced blood supply
patient_explanation: An area of tissue may have been damaged because of reduced blood flow.
clinical_context: In brain reports, this often refers to stroke-related damage.
action_note: A doctor can explain whether it is new or old and what it means.
follow_up: Neurology review; antiplatelet or anticoagulation depending on cause

simple_meaning: Shrinkage or loss of tissue volume
patient_explanation: Some tissue appears smaller than expected.
clinical_context: In brain imaging, mild atrophy can be age-related.
action_note: A doctor can explain if this is expected for age or needs follow-up.
follow_up: Neuropsychological assessment if symptomatic

patient_explanation: Small bony outgrowths have formed at the edges of a joint.
clinical_context: A response to cartilage loss or wear-and-tear. Common with age.
action_note: Usually age-related; physiotherapy or pain management may help.
follow_up: Pain management; physiotherapy; rarely surgery

patient_explanation: One of the cushioning discs between spinal bones has bulged out of place.
clinical_context: Most common in lower spine. Can press on nerves and cause sciatica-like symptoms.
action_note: Neurosurgical or orthopaedic review may be needed depending on symptoms.
follow_up: Physiotherapy; pain management; surgery if neurological deficit

patient_explanation: Too much cerebrospinal fluid has built up inside the brain cavities.
clinical_context: Can increase pressure in the brain and may need urgent assessment.
action_note: This usually requires urgent neurosurgical assessment.
follow_up: Neurosurgical review; shunt may be needed

patient_explanation: An area of bone appears to be breaking down or being eaten away.
clinical_context: Can be aggressive and requires further assessment to find the cause.
action_note: This needs urgent follow-up with a specialist.
follow_up: Oncology; bone biopsy; whole body staging

patient_explanation: Part of the lung looks hazy, like frosted glass, while structures underneath may still be seen.
clinical_context: Often reflects infection, inflammation, edema, or scarring depending on context.
action_note: Follow-up imaging or clinical correlation may be needed.
follow_up: Repeat CT in 3-6 months if persistent; PFTs

patient_explanation: The lymph nodes appear larger than normal.
clinical_context: Can occur with infection, inflammation, immune conditions, or cancer.
action_note: The cause needs to be determined with further tests.
follow_up: Blood tests; biopsy if persistent or large

patient_explanation: The liver appears larger than expected on the scan.
clinical_context: Can be associated with fatty liver, hepatitis, heart failure, or infiltration.
action_note: Liver function tests and further imaging are usually advised.
follow_up: LFTs; hepatology referral if persistent

patient_explanation: The spleen appears larger than normal on the scan.
clinical_context: Can be linked with infection, liver disease, or blood conditions.
action_note: Blood tests and cause evaluation may be needed.
follow_up: FBC; haematology or gastroenterology referral

patient_explanation: Stones have been found inside the gallbladder.
clinical_context: Often incidental unless causing pain or inflammation.
action_note: If asymptomatic, may not need treatment. See a doctor if you have pain.
follow_up: Surgical review if symptomatic; cholecystectomy

patient_explanation: A stone has been found in the kidney or urinary tract.
clinical_context: Most small stones pass naturally. Obstruction changes urgency.
action_note: Pain management and urology review if large.
follow_up: Urology review if >5mm or obstructing; hydration

simple_meaning: Swelling of the kidney due to blocked urine flow
patient_explanation: Urine is not draining properly from the kidney, causing it to swell.
clinical_context: Can happen due to stone, stricture, tumour, enlarged prostate, or pregnancy.
action_note: Urology review is needed especially if bilateral or worsening.
follow_up: Urology; nephrostomy if severe; treat underlying cause

patient_explanation: The main blood vessel in the body has ballooned or bulged in one area.
clinical_context: Size and symptoms determine urgency. Large aneurysms may need repair.
action_note: Urgent vascular surgery review if large or symptomatic.
follow_up: Vascular surgery review; surveillance or repair depending on size

patient_explanation: A blood clot has blocked one of the arteries supplying the lungs.
clinical_context: CTPA is commonly used. Requires urgent medical treatment.
action_note: This requires urgent medical treatment with blood thinners.
follow_up: Anticoagulation; haematology if recurrent

simple_meaning: Blood clot in a deep vein
patient_explanation: A blood clot has formed in one of the deep veins, usually in the leg.
clinical_context: Doppler ultrasound is diagnostic. Untreated clots can travel to the lungs.
action_note: Requires urgent blood thinners to prevent clot spreading to lungs.
follow_up: Anticoagulation; thrombophilia screen if recurrent

simple_meaning: Narrowing of a blood vessel or passage
patient_explanation: A blood vessel or opening has become narrower than normal, reducing flow through it.
clinical_context: Significance depends on location and degree of narrowing.
action_note: The degree of narrowing determines urgency; discuss with your doctor.
follow_up: Angioplasty, stenting, or surgery depending on site and severity

simple_meaning: Area that lights up with contrast dye
patient_explanation: After contrast dye was injected, this area appeared brighter.
clinical_context: Enhancement helps doctors assess blood supply or abnormal tissue behavior.
action_note: Enhancement pattern helps characterise the abnormality.
follow_up: Biopsy or specialist review depending on location

patient_explanation: Too much fat has accumulated in the liver tissue.
clinical_context: Common finding that can improve with lifestyle change.
action_note: Lifestyle changes such as diet and exercise are important.
follow_up: LFTs; hepatology if advanced; weight loss programme

patient_explanation: Free fluid has collected inside the abdomen.
clinical_context: Can be due to liver disease, heart failure, cancer, or infection.
action_note: The cause needs to be investigated.
follow_up: Paracentesis; hepatology or oncology referral

patient_explanation: The tube that drains the pancreas appears wider than normal.
clinical_context: Can happen with obstruction, chronic pancreatitis, cystic lesions, stones, or tumour.
action_note: Specialist review needed to exclude serious causes.
follow_up: MRCP; gastroenterology; oncology if malignancy suspected

simple_meaning: Free air in the abdomen — possible organ perforation
patient_explanation: Air has been found in an area where it should not normally be.
clinical_context: Can suggest pneumoperitoneum and possible bowel or stomach perforation.
action_note: This is a surgical emergency; urgent review needed.
follow_up: Urgent surgical review; laparotomy may be required

patient_explanation: The outer working layer of the kidney appears thinner than normal.
clinical_context: Can indicate chronic kidney disease or long-standing obstruction.
action_note: Kidney function blood tests are needed.
follow_up: Renal function tests; nephrology referral

patient_explanation: One of the spinal bones has slipped forward over the bone below it.
clinical_context: Can cause back pain or nerve symptoms depending on severity.
action_note: Physiotherapy and pain management are usually first-line.
follow_up: Physiotherapy; neurosurgery if neurological deficit

patient_explanation: An area of tissue or bone has become harder or denser than normal.
clinical_context: In joints this is often from arthritis; elsewhere significance depends on context.
action_note: Context determines significance; discuss with your doctor.
follow_up: Depends on context; often incidental in osteoarthritis

patient_explanation: A small sac filled with fluid was seen on the scan. These are usually harmless.
clinical_context: Simple cysts are usually benign. Complex cysts may need follow-up.
action_note: Simple cysts are almost always benign. Complex ones need review.
follow_up: Surveillance if complex; no action for simple benign cysts

simple_meaning: Cloudiness around the centre of the lungs
patient_explanation: The area around the centre of the lungs appears hazy or cloudy on the X-ray.
clinical_context: Can be seen with pulmonary edema, pulmonary hypertension, or infection.
action_note: Usually associated with heart or lung conditions; discuss with your doctor.
follow_up: Cardiology review; echocardiogram may be needed

patient_explanation: The intestine appears blocked, preventing normal passage of contents.
clinical_context: Can cause abdominal pain, vomiting, swelling, and inability to pass gas/stool.
action_note: This requires urgent surgical evaluation.
follow_up: Surgical review; NG tube; IV fluids; possible surgery
